items.correct: A tax set equal to the external cost of an activity — designed to internalise the externality so the private cost reflects the social cost. Used for pollution, congestion, sugary drinks.
items.misconception: A Pigouvian tax is a tax that funds government spending.
items.random_wrong: A Pigouvian tax is a tax only on rich people.
items.why: Pigouvian taxes are corrective, not revenue-raising. The aim is to align private incentives with social welfare. Students treat all taxes as the same (revenue), missing the distinct purpose of Pigouvian intervention.

items.q: What is the predicted effect of a binding price ceiling like rent control?
items.correct: Shortage in the market — quantity demanded exceeds quantity supplied at the controlled price, leading to queues, black markets and reduced quality.
items.misconception: Price ceilings make goods cheaper for everyone with no downsides.
items.random_wrong: Price ceilings only affect the rich.
items.why: Students see price control as obviously beneficial. Standard analysis predicts shortages and unintended consequences — the trade-off is central to evaluation questions on intervention.

items.correct: The change from one additional unit — marginal cost is the cost of producing one more unit; marginal utility is the satisfaction from one more unit. Marginal is incremental, not total or average.
items.misconception: Marginal cost is the total cost of producing all units.
items.why: 'Marginal' has a specific technical meaning: the next unit. Profit maximisation, optimal consumption and many other results depend on marginal — not total or average — quantities. Students conflate the three measures.
Correct answer: An ad valorem indirect tax. “A direct tax” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: When demand is inelastic, the tax burden falls mainly on:
items.why: Correct answer: Consumers. “Producers” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
Exact answer: 20. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
